research_answer: Namibia has actually been diversifying its export base at a rate above the average country given its productive capabilities — outperforming most of its peers on this measure. The country has successfully entered products adjacent to its existing capabilities. However, the quality of this diversification is a major concern: of all the value added generated by products Namibia entered since 2003, a striking 95% corresponds to products with a PCI below the global mean. The new products are mostly in transport, metals, and stone — sectors that are relatively low in complexity. So while Namibia is diversifying, it is diversifying sideways rather than upward along the complexity ladder, adding breadth in low-complexity space rather than breaking into the more sophisticated products that tend to support sustained, inclusive growth.
supporting_quotes: Over the previous decades, Namibia has been able to diversify into products which are adjacent to its exiting capabilities. As a matter of fact, the country has been able to diversify differentially more than the average country and more that most of its peers, given its current set of productive capabilities.
Out of the products added since 2003, 95% of the value added corresponds to products with an average PCI lower than the global mean, mostly transport, metals, and stone products.

data_points.metric: Share of new products' value added with below-median PCI
